title: Dog Sudden Inability to Walk on Back Legs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Sudden hind limb paralysis in dogs is a high-priority emergency. Learn about IVDD, symptoms, and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
What is Sudden Hind Limb Paralysis?

Sudden inability to walk on the back legs, known as hind limb paresis or paralysis, occurs when there is a disruption in the communication between the brain and the rear limbs. This condition often manifests as dragging the legs, 'knuckling' over on the paws, or a complete collapse of the hindquarters. Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal trauma, or a 'spinal stroke' known as Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE).

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-urgency medical emergency. Loss of mobility in the back legs suggests significant spinal cord compression or neurological damage. Time is of the essence; the sooner a dog receives treatment—often involving emergency surgery or intensive anti-inflammatory therapy—the better their prognosis for recovery. If your dog cannot walk, please proceed to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so, capturing a quick video of your dog attempting to move can provide critical information to the triage team. Veterinarians look for specific neurological cues, such as the position of the paws and the dog's ability to support weight, to determine the severity of the injury. Visual documentation allows the medical team to assess the urgency level and prepare the necessary diagnostics before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of sudden back leg failure?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE), and sudden spinal cord trauma or injury.

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from sudden paralysis?
faqs.answer: Many dogs recover with prompt veterinary intervention, which may include surgery, medication, and physical therapy, depending on the specific diagnosis and how quickly they receive care.

faqs.question: How should I transport a paralyzed dog?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog's spine as straight and still as possible. Use a flat board, a sturdy blanket as a stretcher, or a supportive sling to minimize movement and prevent further spinal damage during transport.
